name: Kentland Federal Savings and Loan Association
description_short: America's smallest bank ($3M in assets) founded in 1920, serving Northwest Indiana with community-focused mortgage lending, savings accounts, and CDs for underserved rural markets.
description_long:
Kentland Federal Savings & Loan Association was founded in 1920 and remains one of the most unique financial institutions in America. It operates from the same building where it was established over a century ago and has been family-run for four generations by the Sammons family. The bank is explicitly marketed as "America's Smallest Bank" with only $3 million in assets, positioning itself as a niche community lender rather than a competitive national or regional player.

The bank offers residential mortgages, savings accounts, and certificates of deposit (CDs) as its primary products. Their lending focuses on helping first-time homebuyers and community members achieve homeownership, with all investments directed toward helping residents purchase homes. They position themselves as a local alternative to larger institutions, emphasizing that their money stays in the community and serves local people. The bank also operates financial literacy programs designed to help underserved populations understand deposit accounts, business planning, and credit score improvement.

Kentland Federal distinguishes itself through explicit community mission alignment and values-driven operations. The institution operates as a mutual bank, meaning it prioritizes customer service over corporate profit extraction. Their seven core values—Be Involved, Be Ethical, Be Open, Be Honest, Be Friendly, Be Fair, Be True to Your Word—are foundational to operations. The bank specifically targets economically disadvantaged and historically underserved segments in rural Northwest Indiana, framing their mission around community development rather than growth or market share.

However, prospective customers should understand significant limitations. With only $3 million in assets, this bank cannot offer the product breadth, technology infrastructure, or convenience features of mainstream institutions. There is no mention of checking accounts, online banking, mobile apps, ATM networks, or business loans. The extremely small size may also raise questions about operational sustainability and whether the bank can weather financial downturns. This institution is best suited for customers whose primary goal is supporting a community-focused lender and who have simple financial needs aligned with mortgages or savings products.
services: Residential mortgage loans for home purchase
Financial literacy courses and education programs
Credit score improvement guidance and consulting
Business planning assistance through financial literacy offerings
Community development and underserved market lending
pros: Genuine 100+ year history of community commitment with four generations of family ownership, not a recent rebranding
Explicit mutual bank structure prioritizing customers over corporate shareholders and profit extraction
Highly specialized mortgage lending program helping first-time homebuyers in underserved rural markets
Built-in financial literacy program offering courses on credit scores, business planning, and deposit accounts
Seven clearly articulated core values (honesty, fairness, ethics, community involvement) formally adopted by board
Transparent mission statement focused on economically disadvantaged populations and rural market development
FDIC-insured deposits providing full deposit protection up to insurance limits
cons: Extremely limited product line—no checking accounts, business loans, credit cards, or investment services mentioned
No online banking, mobile app, or digital service infrastructure described; likely minimal technology capabilities
Only $3 million in assets creates significant operational constraints and sustainability concerns for long-term reliability
Geographic limitation to Northwest Indiana with no indication of remote account opening or multi-state service
No mention of ATM network, wire transfers, bill pay, or other basic banking conveniences available at mainstream banks
best_for: First-time homebuyers in Northwest Indiana seeking mortgage lending from a community-focused lender
Rural residents and economically disadvantaged populations wanting to bank locally with transparent community values
Customers prioritizing mission alignment and community development over product variety and digital convenience
Individuals seeking simple savings accounts and CDs with FDIC protection from a values-driven institution
diagnosis: Kentland Federal Savings & Loan is best for customers in Northwest Indiana with modest financial needs (mortgages, savings, CDs) who explicitly value community mission, family ownership, and values-driven banking over product breadth and modern digital services. The primary caveat is that this is an ultra-specialized niche bank with only $3 million in assets, no online banking infrastructure mentioned, and a product line limited to mortgages and deposit accounts—making it unsuitable for customers needing comprehensive banking services or remote access.
typical_results_timeline: Account opening typically takes 1-3 business days. Loan decisions vary by product.

places_enrichment.adr_format_address: 116 N 3rd St, Kentland, IN 47951-1212, USA
